slides.title: Python Reads Like a Recipe
slides.body: Python runs your code **top to bottom**, one line at a time — like a chef following a recipe step by step. No skipping ahead, no going back.
slides.tip: This simple rule explains most beginner errors before you even run the code.

slides.title: Lines Run in Order
slides.body: Each `print()` fires in the exact order it appears. Python finishes line 1, then line 2, then line 3.
slides.code:
print("Step 1: Mix")
print("Step 2: Bake")
print("Step 3: Cool")
slides.output:
Step 1: Mix
Step 2: Bake
Step 3: Cool
slides.tip: Try swapping two lines — the output order changes too.

slides.title: Errors Stop Everything
slides.body: When Python hits a broken line it **stops immediately**. Lines above it already ran; lines below it never will.
slides.code:
print("This runs")
print(oops)
print("This never runs")
slides.output:
This runs
NameError: name 'oops' is not defined
slides.tip: Fix errors from top to bottom — one at a time.

slides.title: Define Before You Use
slides.body: Because Python reads top to bottom, you must **define a variable before** the line that uses it — or you get a `NameError`.
slides.tip: Seeing a NameError? Scroll up — the variable probably appears after the line that needs it.

slides.title: You've Got the Mental Model!
slides.body: Python: top to bottom, one line at a time, stops at errors. That single model makes **error messages less scary** and debugging much faster.
slides.tip: Next up: writing your first real script and watching Python run it live.
